1: Jacob looked up and there was Esau, coming with his four hundred men — the dreaded meeting Jacob had anticipated since Genesis 32. After the night of wrestling with God at Peniel (Genesis 32:24–32), Jacob must face the estranged brother. The wrestling that transformed Jacob prepares him for the encounter with Esau — the same pattern as the Christian life: the encounter with God in prayer and struggle equips believers for reconciliation with those who have wronged them (Matthew 5:23–24: “first go and be reconciled to your brother”). The four hundred men who accompany Esau make the encounter potentially lethal; Jacob’s approach requires the courage that comes from having met God face to face.
2: He put the female servants and their children in front, Leah and her children next, and Rachel and Joseph in the rear — Jacob arranges the family in an order that protects the most beloved last. The self-protective instinct that places the least-beloved first is a reminder that even the covenant-bearer’s love is partial and self-concerned. Christ’s love for his people is precisely the opposite: “He himself went ahead” (v3) — the shepherd places himself first between the flock and danger (John 10:11: “The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep”). Where Jacob placed the most vulnerable first to protect the most beloved, Christ places himself first to protect the most vulnerable.
3: He himself went on ahead and bowed down to the ground seven times as he approached his brother — Jacob goes first — in front of the women and children he had placed behind him — and bows seven times (the number of completeness). The covenant-bearer’s prostration before the brother he wronged is the type of Christ’s kenosis: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death” (Philippians 2:8). The one who has every right to stand upright voluntarily prostrates himself. Seven prostrations — complete, unreserved humiliation — is the type of the complete self-emptying of the incarnation: “he made himself nothing, taking the form of a servant” (Philippians 2:7).
4: But Esau ran to meet Jacob and embraced him; he threw his arms around his neck and kissed him. And they wept — one of the most emotionally charged reconciliation scenes in the OT, and the direct model for Jesus’s parable of the prodigal son: “But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him” (Luke 15:20). The verbal and gestural parallels are nearly exact: running, throwing the arms around the neck, kissing, weeping. The reconciliation of estranged brothers (Jacob the deceiver received by Esau the wronged) is the type of the divine welcome of the repentant sinner: the God who runs to meet the returning one is the God revealed in Christ. The parable’s running father is modeled on Esau’s running embrace — this is the face of God that Jesus shows.
5: “Who are these with you?” Jacob answered, “They are the children God has graciously given your servant” — Jacob’s attribution of his children to God’s grace (“graciously given” is the verb hanan, grace). The children as gift of divine grace — not achievement but grace — is the pattern of the new covenant: “children born not of natural descent… but born of God” (John 1:13). The church as the children God has graciously given to Christ is explicit in John 17:6: “I have revealed you to those whom you gave me out of the world. They were yours; you gave them to me.” Christ can say to the Father about the church what Jacob says to Esau about his children: these are those you graciously gave me.
6: The female servants and their children approached and bowed down — the covenant family’s approach to the one they feared as a threat, now received in peace. The approach of those who were vulnerable — the servants, the children, then the wives — to the one who could have been a destroyer corresponds to the access that Christ’s reconciliation opens: “in him and through faith in him we may approach God with freedom and confidence” (Ephesians 3:12). The fearful approach becomes bold access through the prior reconciliation. Esau’s embrace of Jacob makes the approach of the household possible; Christ’s reconciliation with the Father makes the church’s approach to God possible.
7: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down. Last of all came Joseph and Rachel, and they too bowed down — all of Jacob’s household bows before Esau in an act of covenant submission. The bowing of Joseph and Rachel before Esau foreshadows Joseph’s later experience: the dreams of Genesis 37 show sheaves and stars bowing to Joseph — and this scene of Joseph himself bowing (with his household) before Esau prefigures the great reversal. The humility that precedes exaltation is the pattern of Christ: “he humbled himself… therefore God exalted him” (Philippians 2:8–9). Joseph and Rachel bowing now; Joseph exalted later — the Christ-pattern of descent and ascent.
8: Esau asked, “What’s the meaning of all these flocks and herds I met?” — Esau encounters the gift-procession Jacob had sent ahead (Genesis 32:13–21). Jacob’s gifts sent ahead to “appease” Esau are the OT gift of reconciliation: the offender sending gifts to the offended before the encounter. The NT equivalent is Christ’s atonement as the prior reconciliation that enables the meeting: “we were reconciled to him through the death of his Son” (Romans 5:10) — the reconciliation was achieved before we sought it. God’s gift of Christ (the prior gift) precedes our encounter with him, just as Jacob’s gifts preceded his encounter with Esau.
9: Esau said, “I already have plenty, my brother. Keep what you have for yourself” — Esau’s gracious refusal of the gift he did not need. The one who has been wronged who says “I have enough” is the picture of grace rather than transactional justice: Esau could demand restitution for the stolen blessing but instead declares sufficiency. The God who does not need anything from us but receives us anyway is the NT posture: “The God who made the world and everything in it… is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything” (Acts 17:24–25). Grace does not need to be appeased by our gifts; grace already has plenty and meets us out of abundance, not need.
10: “No, please!” said Jacob. “If I have found favor in your eyes, accept this gift from me. For to see your face is like seeing the face of God, now that you have received me favorably” — “to see your face is like seeing the face of God” — one of the most theologically loaded statements in Genesis. After his experience of wrestling God at Peniel (“I saw God face to face,” Genesis 32:30), Jacob now sees God’s face in the face of his reconciled brother. The face of God is revealed in the act of unexpected forgiveness and acceptance. John 1:18: “No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known.” 2 Corinthians 4:6: “the light of the knowledge of God’s glory displayed in the face of Christ.” The face of God Jacob sees in Esau’s reconciling reception is fully revealed in Christ’s face — the face of God’s grace toward the sinner.
11: “Please accept the blessing I have brought you, for God has been gracious to me and I have all I need.” And because Jacob insisted, Esau accepted it — Jacob returns a blessing to Esau — the reversal of Genesis 27 where he stole the blessing. The restoration of the blessing to the one from whom it was taken is the type of Christ’s restorative work: “I restore what I did not steal” (Psalms 69:4, applied typologically to Christ). Jacob restores what he did steal; Christ restores the blessing to humanity — the blessing forfeited in the fall — though Christ himself stole nothing. The gracious giving of blessing to the one who was wronged is the shape of the gospel: God does not merely forgive the debt but gives the inheritance of blessing to those who forfeited it.
12: Then Esau said, “Let us be on our way; I’ll accompany you” — Esau’s offer of escort: the reconciled enemy who becomes companion for the journey. The protection of the covenant-bearer by the very one who had threatened him is the type of God turning enemies into instruments of protection: “When a man’s ways please the LORD, he makes even his enemies to be at peace with him” (Proverbs 16:7). Christ prayed for his enemies and from the cross said, “Father, forgive them” (Luke 23:34) — the ultimate act of turning enemies into companions. The eschatological vision includes former enemies walking together in peace: “They will beat their swords into plowshares” (Isaiah 2:4).
13: Jacob said to him, “My lord knows that the children are tender and that I must care for the ewes and cows that are nursing their young” — Jacob’s pastoral concern for the tender and the nursing: he cannot move at Esau’s military pace. The shepherd who adjusts the pace of travel to the weakest members of the flock is the type of Christ’s pastoral patience. Isaiah 40:11: “He tends his flock like a shepherd: He gathers the lambs in his arms and carries them close to his heart; he gently leads those that have young.” The same verse pattern is reflected here: Jacob leads gently because of the nursing ewes; the Good Shepherd leads gently because of those who are weak and cannot travel at the pace of the strong.
14: “So let my lord go on ahead of his servant, while I move along slowly at the pace of the flocks and herds before me and the pace of the children” — Jacob moves at the pace of the slowest — the children and the nursing animals. The leader who sets the pace not by his own capability but by the neediest in his care is the type of Christ’s ministry: “A bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not snuff out” (Matthew 12:20, citing Isaiah 42:3). Christ does not demand that the weak move at the pace of the strong; he stoops to the pace of the broken and fragile. The entire incarnation is God moving at the pace of his creature: “the Word became flesh” (John 1:14) — the infinite accommodating itself to the finite’s pace.
15: Esau offered to leave some men with Jacob; Jacob graciously declined — Jacob’s confident refusal of Esau’s armed escort shows that his protection comes from God, not from Esau’s men. The covenant-bearer who trusts divine protection over human protection is the type of the Christian’s confidence: “The LORD is my shepherd, I lack nothing” (Psalms 23:1); “The LORD is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?” (Psalms 27:1). Christ taught the same trust: “do not worry about your life… your heavenly Father feeds them” (Matthew 6:25–26). The covenant-bearer’s gracious declining of human security in favor of divine protection is the posture of faith that Christ embodies and commends.
16: So that day Esau started on his way back to Seir — the separation of Esau and Jacob after the reconciliation. The two brothers part ways after the emotional reunion; Esau returns to Seir (Edom, outside the covenant land) and Jacob continues toward Succoth and then Canaan. The permanent separation of the two lines — Esau to Edom, Jacob to Israel — is the covenant distinction. Paul in Romans 9:13 (citing Malachi 1:2–3: “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated”) develops this separation as the type of divine election: the covenant community is separated from the non-covenant by divine choice, not human achievement — though both are fully human, fully real brothers.
17: Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a place for himself and made shelters for his livestock — Succoth (“shelters/booths”) gives the town its name. The building of shelters (sukkot) connects to the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkoth), which the Torah instituted as a memorial of Israel’s wilderness sheltering (Leviticus 23:42–43). The Feast of Tabernacles in turn becomes the type of the eschatological “tenting” of God with his people: John 1:14 (“the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us” — eskēnōsen, lit. “tabernacled”) and Revelation 21:3 (“God’s dwelling place is now among the people”). Jacob’s shelter at Succoth is a faint type of the divine sheltering that Christ’s incarnation and the eschatological state fully express.
18: Jacob came from Paddan Aram and arrived safely at the city of Shechem in Canaan — the safe return to Canaan after 20 years in Paddan Aram. “Arrived safely” (shālem — whole/complete/at peace) is the arrival of the covenant-bearer back in the covenant land in a state of shalom. The peace-arrival back in the promised land is the type of the ultimate arrival in the promised rest: “There remains, then, a Sabbath-rest for the people of God” (Hebrews 4:9); the journey through exile is finally complete. Christ is the one who arrives in peace — “Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you” (John 14:27) — and who opens the way back to the Father’s presence, the ultimate “Canaan.”
19: He bought from the sons of Hamor the plot of ground where he pitched his tent — Jacob purchases land at Shechem. Stephen mentions this land in his speech: “Jacob went down to Egypt, where he and our ancestors died. Their bodies were brought back to Shechem and placed in the tomb that Abraham had bought” (Acts 7:15–16). The purchased land at Shechem becomes a burial ground — a death-land — before it becomes a resurrection-land (for the people of God who inherit the earth, Matthew 5:5). The covenant-bearer’s purchase of a small plot in the promised land is the down payment of the full inheritance: “he gave him no inheritance here, not even enough ground to set his foot on. But God promised him that he and his descendants after him would possess the land” (Acts 7:5).
20: There he set up an altar and called it El Elohe Israel — “God, the God of Israel”. The first altar Jacob builds after his return to Canaan is named with the covenant formula: the personal relationship between God and the man whose name is now Israel. The covenant name “God of Israel” runs through the entire OT (“the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob” — Matthew 22:32; Exodus 3:6; Acts 7:32) and reaches its fullest expression in Christ: “I am the God of your fathers… I have indeed seen the misery of my people… I am sending you” (Acts 7:33–34). The God who names himself by covenant relationship — God-of-Israel — is the same God who says “I am the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ” (Ephesians 1:3; 2 Corinthians 1:3). The altar built with the covenant name is the worship posture of those who know God by his relationship to his people.
